lines:
The gate to the old dungeon had been sealed for longer than anyone in the village could remember, its iron bars fused with rust and its lock long since seized shut around a keyhole nobody had a key for. Which was, naturally, exactly why Mira wanted to open it.

She'd heard the stories the way everyone had: whispered warnings from grandparents, dared-you rumors traded at school, the occasional drunk uncle swearing he'd heard something moving beneath the hill on a still night. None of it had ever stopped her from being curious, and if anything, it had only made the gate more interesting.

The torch in her hand sputtered as she knelt by the lock, running her fingers over metal gone rough with age. Somewhere behind that door, she was certain, was more than just old stone and cobwebs. Dungeons like this one didn't get sealed for nothing, and sealed things had a way of being worth finding, whatever else they might also be.

The lock gave with a groan that seemed to come from the hinges themselves rather than the metal, and the gate swung inward on its own, as though it, too, had been waiting. Cool air rushed out to meet her, carrying with it the unmistakable smell of somewhere old and mostly forgotten.

Mira took a breath, gripped her torch a little tighter, and stepped through.

Somewhere below, something enormous and slow shifted in its sleep, disturbed for the first time in a very long while.

The city had built its walls higher every generation, layer upon layer of stone stacked by hands that never quite believed the old warnings, until the walls themselves had become taller than the temple spires they were meant to protect.

Nobody living had seen the thing the walls were built against. That was rather the point. Generations came and went, wars were fought and forgotten, and the old stories about a sleeping horror beneath the southern hills were filed alongside every other tale told to keep children from wandering too far after dark.

Which made it all the more surprising when the ground began to shake on an otherwise unremarkable Tuesday.

At first, people assumed it was an earthquake, and went about securing their shelves and calming their animals, the way anyone would. It was only when the shaking didn't stop, when it instead grew steady and rhythmic like something enormous taking its first slow steps in centuries, that anyone thought to look south.

By then, the walls didn't look quite so tall anymore.
